coredata.dc:title: The effect of CQDs’ particle size on its fluorescence behavior and Cu2+ detection
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Ying
Feng, Shang
Zhu, Qiufeng
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have emerged as promising fluorescent sensors for ion detection due to their tunable optical properties. In this study, CQDs with systematically controlled particle sizes were synthesized via a hydrothermal method to investigate the effect of size on their fluorescence properties and Cu2+ detection capabilities. Characterization techniques, including transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), and spectroscopic analysis, revealed that the smaller CQDs particles (<3 nm) exhibited the weaker fluorescence intensity and shorter emission wavelength compared to those of the larger CQDs particles (>3 nm). These phenomena were attributed to quantum confinement effects and surface state modulation, where reduced particle size enhanced quantum confinement, leading to discrete energy levels and blue-shifted emission. However, CQDs with smaller particle size was easy to aggregate in solution because of its high surface energy, which made it difficult to achieve system stability, thus interfering with the detection accuracy. The optimized CQDs demonstrated high sensitivity for Cu2+ detection with a linear response in the concentration range of 0–3 mM and a detection limit as low as 0.09 nM. Mechanism studies indicated that Cu2+-induced fluorescence quenching followed a dynamic quenching process, where size-dependent surface charge and functional groups (e.g., hydroxyl, carboxyl) influenced the binding affinity between CQDs and Cu2+. Smaller CQDs, despite their aggregation tendency, exhibited stronger electrostatic interactions with Cu2+ due to their higher surface-to-volume ratio, enhancing detection specificity. This work highlighted the critical role of particle size in tailoring CQDs’ optical properties and sensing performance, providing insights for designing efficient fluorescent probes for environmental monitoring and ion analysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CQDs
Optimize
Fluorescence properties
Cu2+

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Morphology and structure analyses of CQDs
Detection of Cu2+ in actual tap water

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of CQDs-T160 (a), CQDs-T180 (b), N-CQDs (c), CQDs-t2 (d), CQDs-t6(e), with inset size distribution histograms.
XPS spectrum (a) and high-resolution C (b), N (c) and O (d) spectra of CQDs-T160, CQDs-T180, N-CQDs, CQDs-t2, and CQDs-t6.
The FT-IR spectra of series CQDs.
The zeta point profiles (a), PL intensity at different pH values (b) of CQDs.
The calibration curves of Cu2+ with CQDs.
The fluorescence lifetimes of CQDs solution before and after adding Cu2+.
Interference experiments (a) and the fluorescence quenching by Fe3+ and Cu2+ (b), Fluorescence quenching intensity peak spectra of N-CQDs in a mixed solution of Cu2+ and Fe3+(c).
The calibration curves of Cu2+ and N-CQD mixed for different times (10 min, 70 min, 120 min).

head.ce:title: The effect of CQDs’ particle size on its fluorescence behavior and Cu2+ detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Light Industry Science and Engineering, Beijing Technology and Business University, Beijing 100048, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Light Industry Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Light Industry Science and Engineering, Beijing Technology and Business University, Beijing 100048, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Cleaner Production and Integrated Resource Utilization of China National Light Industry, Beijing 100048, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Cleaner Production and Integrated Resource Utilization of China National Light Industry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Cleaner Production and Integrated Resource Utilization of China National Light Industry, Beijing 100048, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Light Industry Science and Engineering, Beijing Technology and Business University, Beijing 100048, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Light Industry Science and Engineering

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: The authors have contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have emerged as promising fluorescent sensors for ion detection due to their tunable optical properties. In this study, CQDs with systematically controlled particle sizes were synthesized via a hydrothermal method to investigate the effect of size on their fluorescence properties and Cu2+ detection capabilities. Characterization techniques, including transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), and spectroscopic analysis, revealed that the smaller CQDs particles (<3 nm) exhibited the weaker fluorescence intensity and shorter emission wavelength compared to those of the larger CQDs particles (>3 nm). These phenomena were attributed to quantum confinement effects and surface state modulation, where reduced particle size enhanced quantum confinement, leading to discrete energy levels and blue-shifted emission. However, CQDs with smaller particle size was easy to aggregate in solution because of its high surface energy, which made it difficult to achieve system stability, thus interfering with the detection accuracy. The optimized CQDs demonstrated high sensitivity for Cu2+ detection with a linear response in the concentration range of 0–3 mM and a detection limit as low as 0.09 nM. Mechanism studies indicated that Cu2+-induced fluorescence quenching followed a dynamic quenching process, where size-dependent surface charge and functional groups (e.g., hydroxyl, carboxyl) influenced the binding affinity between CQDs and Cu2+. Smaller CQDs, despite their aggregation tendency, exhibited stronger electrostatic interactions with Cu2+ due to their higher surface-to-volume ratio, enhancing detection specificity. This work highlighted the critical role of particle size in tailoring CQDs’ optical properties and sensing performance, providing insights for designing efficient fluorescent probes for environmental monitoring and ion analysis.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Heavy metals in the environment have become a global concern due to their potential risks to ecosystems and human health. Copper (Cu2+), a prominent heavy metal element, is extensively utilized in various industrial sectors, including electronics manufacturing and metal plating [1]. As a result, due to the discharge of industrial wastewaters containing copper and the gradual leaching of copper from products and infrastructure, Cu2+ is ubiquitous in the natural environment and wastewater [2]. Conventionally, numerous analytical methods have been developed to quantify Cu2+ in various media. These include flame atomic absorption spectroscopy [3], graphite furnace atomic absorption spectroscopy [4], and inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry [5,6]. Nevertheless, these methods require costly instrumentation and complex operations, rendering them unsuitable for convenient and expeditious detection. In contrast, Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have emerged as a promising alternative [7].

As the simplest form of carbon dots, CQDs typically consist of ultra-fine, quasi-spherical carbon nanoparticles with a size of less than 10 nm. These zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials are distinguished by their significant fluorescence characteristics, accompanied by a suite of advantageous attributes such as unique optical traits, excellent water solubility, high stability, and minimal environmental impact [8–10]. Leveraging these merits, CQDs have found extensive applications in chemical sensing [7], photoelectric devices [11], biological imaging [12], and other related fields. Notably, when employed as a fluorescent sensor for Cu2+ detection, CQDs overcome the inherent drawbacks of traditional detection modalities. Since their initial discovery in 2004 [7], extensive research efforts have been dedicated to exploring CQDs’ synthesis pathways, physicochemical properties, and practical applications, leading to substantial theoretical and experimental progress [13–15].

In fact, the fluorescent behavior of CQDs exhibits a pronounced susceptibility to changes in their size. Variations in size lead to differences in the number of sp 2 fragments and the surface area of the CQDs, which in turn, profoundly influence their fluorescence characteristics [16]. For example, Zheng’s group [9] synthesized three functionalized CDs with distinct sizes, each showing significantly enhanced fluorescence responses to Cr6+, Cu2+, and Fe3+ ions, respectively. Similarly, S. Sarkar’s group [10] studied the size-dependent photoluminescence property of hydrothermally synthesized crystalline CQDs, and Zhou’s group [17] investigated the size-dependent photocatalytic activity of CQDs with surface-state.

Doping CQDs with heteroatoms, particularly N and S, represents an efficacious strategy for improving their fluorescence behavior. This is predicated on the fact that the atomic radii and orbitals of these heteroatoms are similar to those of carbon. Ren’s group [18] synthesized the nitrogen-rich CQDs capable of simultaneously detecting and remediating trace Co2+ under saline conditions. Chen’s group [19] undertook a study on one-step hydrothermal synthesis of N-doped fluorescent CQDs from fermented rice, which exhibited highly selective characteristics for label-free detection of Fe3+ ions and served as fluorescent ink for photoluminescence determination. Wang’s group [20] fabricated the N, S- Co-doped CQDs with highly luminescent and applied them for detecting mercury(II). Additionally, Zhang’s group [21] developed a label-free fluorescent sensor based on Si, N-Co doped CQDs with enhanced sensitivity for the determination of Cr(VI).

In the present study, citric acid was selected as the precursor, and urea, a commonly available nitrogen-containing compound, was employed for synthesizing CQDs via a one-step hydrothermal synthesis method. By meticulously adjusting the synthesis conditions, CQDs with excellent fluorescence properties were obtained. These properties are predominantly dictated by the size of the CQDs, which in turn, causes changes in their absorption and emission spectra. It was found that the larger CQDs (>3 nm) exhibited stronger fluorescence intensity and a higher quantum yield compared to smaller particles (<3 nm), which could be attributed to quantum confinement effects and surface state modification. The optimized CQDs demonstrated high sensitivity for Cu2+ detection, with a linear response in the concentration range of 0–3 mM and a detection limit of 8.7 nM. This research provided a new perspective and method for the detection of Cu2+ in environmental samples.

The urea [CH4N2O], calcium chloride [CaCl2], magnesium chloride [MgCl2] and ferric chloride [FeCl3] were provided by Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co. Ltd. Citric acid [C6H8O7·H2O], manganese sulfate [MnSO4·H2O] were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. Copper nitrate trihydrate [Cu(NO3)2·3H2O], zinc nitrate hexahydrate [Zn(NO3)2·6H2O], sodium chloride [NaCl(99.5 %)] and ammonium sulfate [(NH4)2SO4] were supplied by the Tianjin Guangfu Technology Development Co. Ltd. Potassium sulfate [K2SO4] was purchased from Xinhua Chemical Plant in Tanggu, Tianjin. Barium hydroxide [Ba(OH)2] was obtained from Fuchen (Tianjin) Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd, and anhydrous ethanol (CH3CH2OH) was sourced from Beijing Tongguang Company.

A series of CQDs were synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal method by varying the preparation conditions. Initially, citric acid and urea were dissolved in ultra-pure water. the resulting solution was then transferred to an autoclave and heated in a drying oven at a specific temperature. After allowing the autoclave to cool down to ambient temperature, the CQDs solution was filtered through a 0.22 μ m membrane. Subsequently, it was mixed with anhydrous ethanol and centrifuged at 5000 r/min for 10 min. Finally, the CQDs powder was obtained by drying in a vacuum oven at 60 ℃.

The CQDs samples were prepared with a citrate-urea ratio of 1 and a hydrothermal duration of 4 h were assigned as CQDs-Tn, where “n” represented different reaction temperatures (160, 180 and 200 ℃), For example, CQDs-T160 indicated a reaction temperature of 160 ℃.

The CQDs samples were obtained from a citrate-urea ratio of 1 and a reaction temperature of 200 ℃ were labeled as CQDs-tm, where “m” represented various hydrothermal reaction duration (2, 4, and 6 h). For instance, CQDs-t4 corresponded to a hydrothermal reaction duration of 4 h.

The CQDs samples were synthesized at a reaction temperature of 200 ℃ and a hydrothermal reaction duration of 4 h was designated as CQDs-Ry, where “y” represented different citrate-urea ratios (0.5, 1, 1.5, and 2). For example, CQDs-R1.5 referred to a citrate-urea ratio of 1.5.
Notably, N-CQDs were prepared using the same method as that for CQDs-t4, CQDs-T200 or CQDs-R1.

Firstly, 10 mg of CQDs powder was dissolved thoroughly in 50 mL of ultra-pure water to ensure homogeneity of the sensing solution. A series of Cu2+ solutions with concentrations varying from 0 to 3.0 mM were meticulously prepared by adding Cu(NO3)2·3H2O into ultra-pure water.

Subsequently, to establish a relationship between the fluorescence intensity of CQDs and the concentration of Cu2+ solution, the two solutions were mixed in a precisely controlled volume ratio of 2:1. Then, the emission spectrum was recorded at an excitation wavelength of 380 nm.

Finally, the quantitative relationship between the fluorescence intensity (F) of the CQDs-Cu2+ mixed solution and the Cu2+ concentration ([Cu2+]) could be accurately modeled and fitted according to Formula (1).
(1)
F =
F 0
+
K
SV
C
u
2 +

In this formula, F 0 represents the initial fluorescence intensity of the CQDs solution, [Cu2+] denotes the different concentrations of Cu2+, and K sv is the well-defined dynamic quenching constant, as referenced in previous literature [22].

The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated based on the standard deviation of the blank measurements and the slope of the calibration curve according to the following Equation (2):
(2)
LOD =
3 σ
S

where σ represents the standard deviation of blank signals, and S is the slope of the linear calibration curve [23]. The factor of 3 is employed to ensure a confidence level of approximately 99 % that the detected signal is statistically distinguishable from background noise. Blank samples were analyzed in triplicate to determine the standard deviation (σ), and the calibration curve was constructed by plotting the fluorescence intensity against Cu2+ concentration within the linear range of 0–3 mM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and structure analyses of CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The particle size distribution and morphology of CQDs were characterized and analyzed by TEM analysis. As depicted in Fig. 1 , all the CQDs synthesized under the aforementioned conditions displayed a nearly spherical or oval shape, with particle sizes less than 10 nm. Among the samples under comparison, there were no significant differences in shape, except for their average particle size.

As shown in Fig. 1.a-c, for the samples synthesized with different reaction temperatures but the constant hydrothermal time of 4 h and citrate-urea ratio of 1, the particle size of CQDs-T160 was distributed in the range of 1–7 nm, with an average diameter of 2.86 nm. The particle size of CQDs-T180 ranged from 1.5 to 5.5 nm, with a mean size of 3.44 nm. The nitrogen-doped CQDs (N-CQDs) had a size distribution of 2–6 nm, with an average size of 3.57 nm. Obviously, the particle size of CQDs gradually increased as the hydrothermal temperature rose. Additionally, as shown in Fig. 1.d-e, the size of CQDs-t2 was 2.73 nm and that of CQDs-t6 was 2.63 nm, when the reaction temperature was kept constant at 200℃ and the nitrogen doping ratio was 1. This result indicated that the particle size of the CQDs decreased with the prolongation of hydrothermal time. It was speculated that the increase in reaction temperature and the extension of heating time led to the agglomeration of small-sized CQDs [37].

The elemental composition evolution and surface functional groups of the samples were identified through XPS and FT-IR spectra. In Fig. 2 a, the distinct signals corresponding to C1s, N1s, and O1s were clearly detected. The combined contents of N and O were approximately 50 %, indicating that the synthesized CQDs were N, O-doped, carbon-rich nanodots. For the N-CQDs, the high-resolution C1s spectra (Fig. 2b) revealed the presence of three types of carbon species. Specifically, the peaks at 284.7, 286.1, and 288.6 eV were attributed to sp 2 CC/CC carbon, sp 3 CO carbon and COOH groups, respectively. The N1s band (Fig. 2c) could be deconvoluted into three peaks at 398.4, 399.1, and 408.2 eV, representing pyridinic N, amino N, and pyrrolic N, respectively. As for the high-resolution O1s spectrum (Fig. 2d), three peaks were identified. The peaks at 543.0 eV, 531.6 eV, and 523.8 eV were assigned to OH, CO, and COH functional group, respectively [16,24].

The FT-IR spectra of the synthesized CQDs (CQDs-T160, CQDs-T180, CQDs-T200, CQDs-t2, and CQDs-t6) as well as their precursors (urea and citric acid, CA) were shown in Fig. 3 . Comparing the spectra of CQDs with those of the precursors (urea and CA), it could be observed that many sharp and distinct peaks in urea and citric acid disappeared or broadened after the hydrothermal reaction, confirming the successful carbonization and chemical transformation during the synthesis process. A broad absorption band centered around 3400 cm−1 was observed in all samples, which was attributed to the stretching vibrations of OH and NH groups. This indicated the presence of hydroxyl and amine groups on the CQD surfaces, which enhanced their hydrophilicity and functional reactivity [25,26]. Characteristic absorption peaks around 2850–2970 cm−1 correspond to the CH stretching vibrations of aliphatic groups, suggesting partial retention of carbon-hydrogen bonds during the hydrothermal carbonization [27,28]. The weak absorption features between 2500 and 2000 cm−1 might originate from trace residual precursors (e.g., intermediates of urea decomposition) or conjugated structures formed during synthesis (e.g., CO or CN) [29,30]. A strong absorption peak at approximately 1700 cm−1 was assigned to the CO stretching vibrations of carbonyl or carboxyl groups [31], while peaks in the 1560–1550 cm−1 region corresponded to CC stretching vibrations, indicating the presence of conjugated aromatic or graphitic domains within the CQDs. Additionally, the absorption bands in the 1200–1000 cm−1 region were attributed to C-O-C (ether bonds) and C-N stretching vibrations [29,31], conforming the successful incorporation of nitrogen functionalities into the CQDs through urea doping. These functional groups on the CQD surface were critical for enhancing their dispersibility, fluorescence properties, and interaction with metal ions such as Cu2+.

Indeed, the CQDs consisted of sp 2/sp 3 carbon, oxygen- or nitrogen-based containing groups, and other modified chemical groups; they exhibited similar optical properties in absorption and fluorescence and showed strong optical absorption in the UV region, with the absorption tail extending into the visible range [32–34]. As shown in Fig. 4 , all samples displayed two distinct absorption peaks at 239 nm and at 331 nm, respectively. The absorption band near 239 nm was attributed to the π–π* transition of CC bonds within the conjugated carbon frameworks of the CQDs. This transition was characteristic of sp 2-hybridized carbon networks, corroborating the presence of graphitic or aromatic domains in the CQDs. The absorption peak around 331 nm corresponded to the n–π* transition of CO bonds [35], suggesting the existence of carbonyl and other oxygen-containing surface functional groups introduced during the hydrothermal synthesis.

In fact, nitrogen doping is the most effective way to optimizing the fluorescence behavior of CQDs and enhancing their quantum yield. In Fig. 5 , the CQDs synthesized from citric acid or urea alone were significantly lower than that of nitrogen-doped CQDs. Moreover, the optimal emission wavelength of doped CQDs exhibited a blue or red shift, indicating the formation of new material, i.e., nitrogen-doped CQDs. The fluorescence behavior of CQDs was strongly correlated with their particle size. Under excitation at 380 nm, the fluorescence emission peaks of the CQDs exhibited distinct shifts. For CQDs particles with size of smaller than 3 nm, the maximum emission peak appeared below 450 nm, showing a slight blue shift relative to that of N-CQDs. This observation highlighted that at the nanoscale, the quantum confinement effect became more pronounced, where smaller CQDs possessed larger band gaps, resulting in emission at shorter wavelengths. With the increase of particle size, the number of surface defects also increased, which might promote nonradiative recombination and induced a red shift in emission [36–38]. Notably, CQDs-t6, the sample with the smallest particle size, exhibited a pronounced red shift, deviating from the expected trend. This anomaly was likely attributed to the aggregation of ultra-small CQDs, where aggregation-induced quenching and red shifts occurred as the system stabilized [39,40]. Consequently, the quantum yield (QYs) of CQDs was relatively low (10–18.4 %) with some specific values of CQDs-T160 = 10.3 %, CQDs-T180 = 11.8 %, N-CQDs = 15.4 %, CQDs-t2 = 10.7 %, CQDs-t6 = 18.4 %. Intriguingly, the lower QYs were correlated with a more sensitive fluorescence response, which facilitated the linear detection of Cu2+.

It was worth emphasizing that achieving uniform particle size distribution during CQD synthesis remained significant challenging. Intermolecular interactions, such as van der Waals forces, could drive the aggregation of smaller CQDs, thereby compromising system stabilization [41]. To investigate the stability of CQDs, their fluorescence behavior was further monitored over a 24-hour period. As shown in Fig. 6 , upon dissolution in water, the fluorescence intensity reached its peak within one hour and then decreased over the subsequent 24 h. Quantitative analysis revealed fluorescence intensity reductions of 25.63 % (CQDs-T160), 10.88 % (CQDs-T180), 9.7 % (N-CQDs), 24.97 % (CQDs-t2), and 14.46 % (CQDs-t6), which were likely attributable to CQDs aggregation-induced quenching. Among these, N-CQDs exhibited superior fluorescence stability and detection accuracy, positioning it as optimal candidates for Cu2+ sensing applications.

Highly electronegative atoms such as oxygen, nitrogen have a stronger tendency to bind with water molecules. As a result, their compounds generally exhibit high hydrophilicity. When the aqueous solution of CQDs was analyzed using a Malvern point potentiometer, the zeta potential values were recorded as follows: −16.21 mV for CQDs-T160, −21.71 mV for CQDs-T180, −24.65 mV for N-CQDs, –23.64 mV for CQDs-t2, and −11.90 mV for CQDs-t6, as shown in Fig. 7 a. Overall, these negative potential values indicated that the CQDs exhibited excellent water solubility. Moreover, the CQDs solution carried a negative charge, rendering it more alkaline. Therefore, it showed robust fluorescence emission in alkaline environments. As depicted in Fig. 7b, the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs was notably high in both neutral and alkaline conditions, while fluorescence quenching occurred under acidic conditions.

As a sensor for Cu2+ detection, the CQDs demonstrated remarkable specific selectivity towards Cu2+. It could effectively quench the fluorescence intensity of CQDs within a mere 1 min. As illustrated in Fig. 8 a-c, for CQDs-T160 with a size of 2.86 nm, the linear correlation coefficient was 0.9679 in the concentration range of 1.0–3.0 mM of Cu2+. CQDs-T180 with the size of 3.44 nm exhibited an R 2 value of 0.9745 in the Cu2+ concentration range of 0.2–0.9 mM and 0.9982 for 1.0–6.0 mM. Notably, the N-CQDs with the size of 3.57 nm could effectively detect Cu2+ over a wide concentration range, with correlation coefficients of 0.9940 (1.0–3.0 mM), 0.9904 (0.1–1.0 mM), 0.9928 (0.01–0.1 mM), 0,9913 (0.0001–0.001 mM), and 0.9803 (0.00001–0.0001 mM) within the of 0–3.0 mM range.

Interestingly, as the concentration of Cu2+ decreased, the experiment revealed a steeper slope between Cu2+ and CQDs, indicating enhanced sensitivity to Cu2+. As shown in Fig. 8d, by comparing the detection results of CQDs-t6, N-CQDs and CQDs-t2, a linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity of CQDs and Cu2+ was established within the range of 0.02–0.07 mM. The correlation coefficients were 0.9826 for CQDs-t6 (2.63 nm), 0.9773 for N-CQDs (3.57 nm), and 0.9507 for CQDs-t2 (2.73 nm), respectively.

Overall, the experiment investigated the impact of CQDs’ size on detection accuracy of Cu2+. Smaller-sized CQDs led to more accurate detection and had a lower limit of detection. In fact, as the particle size of CQDs decreased, their specific surface area and number of surface atoms increased, endowing them with high surface activity and facilitating binding with other atoms, which in turn influenced their optical properties. Consequently, the size-dependent fluorescence quenching effect could be ascribed to the quantity of binding sites on the surface of CQDs, making Cu2+ detection more sensitive with high quenching efficiency. Fortunately, all the calibration coefficients R 2 were above 0.9800, signifying practical application value.

To further investigate the mechanism of fluorescence quenching induced by the interaction between Cu2+ and CQDs, the fluorescence lifetimes of the CQDs were measured before and after the addition of Cu2+. As shown in Fig. 9 , the results showed that the fluorescence lifetime decreased from 6.37 ns to 2.87 ns. This shortening was a typically indicator of dynamic fluorescence quenching [42], suggesting that the excited-state electrons in CQDs were rapidly dissipated through collisions with Cu2+ or that energy was transferred from CQDs to Cu2+ via non-radiative pathways (e.g., energy transfer or electron transfer). Consequently, the Stern-Volmer equation was employed to quantitatively analyze the effect of Cu2+ concentration on the fluorescence properties of CQDs.

The anti-interference ability of a sensor or detection method is an important factor. So, the selectivity of CQDs for various metal ions was investigated by assessing the fluorescence quenching efficiency in the presence of Mg2+, Na+, K+, NH4+, Zn2+, Ba2+, Mn2+, Ca2+, Fe3+, and Cu2+. As illustrated in Fig. 10 a, when the CQDs were used to detect metal ions at a concentration of 10-3 M, it was found that Cu2+ could quench 83.91 % of the initial fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs. In contrast, other metal ions induced slight variations in fluorescence (<4%). Nevertheless, Fe3+ exhibited a much stronger quenching effect on N-CQDs (99.66 %), which significantly interfered with the detection of Cu2+. Fortunately, their fluorescence spectra differed. As presented in Fig. 8b, the emission wavelengths of CQDs mixed with Fe3+ or Cu2+ were clearly distinguishable. The emission wavelength of CQDs-Fe3+ was 465 nm, while that of the CQDs-Cu2+ mixture was 445 nm. This difference was effective in distinguishing between the two metal ions. Nevertheless, this issue still presents a challenge in the fluorescence detection process using CQDs.

Simultaneous detection of Cu2+ and Fe3+ could be achieved by using a peak-resolution strategy. In this experiment, the fluorescence spectra of carbon dot showed distinct emission peaks at 445 nm and 465 nm in the presence of Cu2+ or Fe3+ alone, respectively. The peak shifts arose from changes in electronic transition energy level induced by specific coordination between metal ions and functional groups on the surface of CQDs. When both metal ions existed at the same time, the fluorescence spectra of the mixed solution displayed double-peak superposition (Fig. 10c), and the corresponding peak positions and intensities of the two ions could be resolved via peak fitting. This peak-splitting strategy based on feature peak resolution provided a novel method for multi-component fluorescence sensing, holding significant potential for simultaneous monitoring of heavy metal ions in complex environmental samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Cu2+ in actual tap water
ce:section.ce:para: Eventually, an actual water sample was applied to evaluate the practical utility of the CQDs sensor. In the experiment, laboratory tap water was first centrifuged at 5000 r/min for 10 min. This pre-treated water was then used as a solvent to dissolve Cu2+, within a concentration range of 0.5–3.5 mM. Subsequently, N-CQDs were employed as the detector for the quantitative determination of the metal ion. As depicted in Fig. 11 , the detection of Cu2+ was conducted at 10, 70, and 120 min respectively. In each case, excellent linear relationships were established. The correlation coefficients were calculated as R 1 2 = 0.9615, R 2 2 = 0.9666, and R 3 2 = 0.9565, all of which exceeded 0.9500. This result significantly enhanced the practical application potential of N-CQDs in Cu2+ detection.

This study developed a highly efficient and sensitive method for Cu2+ detection using nitrogen-doped CQDs synthesized from citric acid and urea. By optimizing synthesis conditions (time/temperature), the as-prepared CQDs exhibited excellent water solubility, fluorescence properties, and chemical stability. Smaller CQDs (<3 nm) showed weaker fluorescence intensity and shorter emission wavelengths due to quantum confinement effects and surface state modification, though their tendency to aggregate reduced fluorescence stability. Leveraging dynamic fluorescence quenching, the optimized N-CQDs achieved high sensitivity toward Cu2+ with a linear detection range in the concentration range of 0–3 mM and a detection limit (LOD) as low as 0.09 nM, which was much lower than that of organic dye-based fluorescence probes and some electrochemical sensors, alongside rapid response kinetics and strong selectivity against other usual metal ions. The sensor demonstrated reliable performance in real water samples, highlighting its promise for Cu2+ analysis in environmental monitoring. This work provided new methodological insights for trace metal ion detection in complex matrices.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Potential applications of extremophilic bacteria in the bioremediation of extreme environments contaminated with heavy metals
Environ. Manage

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J.Z. Sun, X. He, Y.L. Le, et al., Potential applications of extremophilic bacteria in the bioremediation of extreme environments contaminated with heavy metals, Environ. Manage 328 (2020) 127119, Doi: 10.1016/j.foodchem.2020.127119.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Copper uptake, essentiality, toxicity, detoxification and risk assessment in soil-plant environment
Chemosphere

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Zunaira, S. Aneeza, S. Abrar, et al., Copper uptake, essentiality, toxicity, detoxification and risk assessment in soil-plant environment, Chemosphere 259 (2020) 127436, Doi: 10.1016/j.chemosphere.2020.127436.
